subsections.title: Big-O & Core Data Structures
subsections.keyRule: Big-O measures how algorithm time/space grows with input size. O(1) = constant. O(log n) = binary search. O(n) = linear scan. O(n log n) = merge/quick sort. O(n²) = nested loops. O(2ⁿ) = exponential. Always know time and space complexity of your solution.

terms.what: Big-O describes the worst-case growth rate of an algorithm. It tells you: if input doubles, how much slower does it get?
terms.why: Choosing an O(n²) algorithm for 1 million records gives 10¹² operations — unusable. O(n log n) gives 20 million — fast.
terms.how: O(1): array access by index. O(log n): binary search (halves input each step). O(n): single loop. O(n log n): merge sort. O(n²): two nested loops. O(2ⁿ): recursive subset enumeration.
terms.where: Every algorithm and data structure choice. Code reviews should flag O(n²) in performance-critical paths.
terms.when: Think about Big-O when: choosing between algorithms, optimizing slow code, justifying technical decisions in interviews.
terms.issue: O(n²) inside a loop that iterates n times = O(n³) — fine for n=100, unusable for n=10,000.
terms.fix: Look for nested loops iterating the same collection. Optimize inner loop with HashMap lookups (O(1)) to reduce O(n²) to O(n).

star.s: Interview: given a list, find all pairs that sum to target.
star.a: O(n²) brute force: nested loops. O(n) optimal: store seen numbers in HashSet. For each number, check if (target - number) in set.

terms.what: Linked list = nodes where each holds data + pointer to next node. No contiguous memory. O(1) insert/remove at known node. O(n) search.
terms.why: Dynamic size. Efficient insert/remove at head (O(1)). No wasted capacity like arrays.
terms.how: Key operations: fast/slow pointer (cycle detection), reverse in-place, find middle, merge two sorted lists.
terms.where: Interview questions on linked lists are very common. Real usage: Java LinkedList, LRU cache (doubly linked list + hashmap).
terms.when: Know the algorithms: reverse, cycle detection (Floyd's algorithm), find middle, merge.
terms.issue: NullPointerException from not checking null before accessing node.next.
terms.fix: Always check: if (node == null) return; before accessing node.next.

star.s: Interview: remove duplicates from sorted array in-place.
star.a: Two pointer — slow pointer tracks last unique. Fast pointer scans. When fast finds new value, advance slow and copy.
star.r: O(n) time, O(1) space. Linked List

terms.what: Binary search finds a target in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search space. O(log n) — extremely fast. 1 billion elements takes max 30 comparisons.
terms.why: Linear search takes O(n) for sorted data — wasted. Binary search uses the sorted property to eliminate half the elements each step.
terms.how: left=0, right=n-1. mid=(left+right)/2. If arr[mid]==target return mid. If target<arr[mid]: right=mid-1. Else: left=mid+1.
terms.where: Sorted array search, finding boundaries (first/last occurrence), search space problems, finding threshold in monotonic functions.
terms.when: Whenever input is sorted, think binary search. Also applies to 'search for answer' problems (binary search the answer space).
terms.issue: Integer overflow in mid = (left+right)/2 when indices are large ints.

star.s: Interview: find first occurrence of target in sorted array with duplicates.
star.a: Standard binary search but on finding target, save result and continue left (right=mid-1).

terms.what: Binary Tree = each node has max 2 children. Binary Search Tree (BST) = left < node < right. Enables O(log n) search, insert, delete when balanced.
terms.why: Hierarchical data, sorted data with fast lookup, file systems, expression parsing.
terms.how: BFS (level order): Queue. DFS: Pre-order (root, left, right), In-order (left, root, right — gives sorted for BST), Post-order (left, right, root). In-order traversal of BST = sorted sequence.
terms.where: File systems, expression trees, autocomplete, database indexes (B-tree variant).
terms.when: Know recursive and iterative DFS. Know BFS with Queue. Know in-order for BST sorted output.
terms.issue: Unbalanced BST degrades to O(n) — like a linked list. AVL trees and Red-Black trees (TreeMap) stay balanced.
terms.fix: Java's TreeMap uses Red-Black Tree — guaranteed O(log n). For interview, know how to check balance and why it matters.

star.s: Interview: validate if a binary tree is a valid BST.
star.a: In- order traversal — collect values. If strictly increasing, valid BST. Or recursive with min/max bounds per node.

terms.what: Dynamic Programming (DP) solves problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and remembering results (memoization/tabulation) — avoid recomputing.
terms.why: Naive recursion for Fibonacci, knapsack, longest common subsequence recomputes same subproblems exponentially. DP reduces to polynomial time.
terms.how: Two approaches: Top-down (memoization — recursive with HashMap cache) and Bottom-up (tabulation — iterative DP table). Fibonacci: dp[n] = dp[n-1] + dp[n-2].
terms.where: Interviews: coin change, knapsack, longest increasing subsequence, edit distance, grid paths. Real world: route optimization, spell check, bioinformatics.
terms.when: Use DP when: problem has optimal substructure (optimal solution uses optimal sub- solutions) and overlapping subproblems.
terms.issue: Recognizing when DP applies is the hard part. Many developers jump to recursion and get TLE on large inputs.
terms.fix: If brute force recursion works but is slow, add memoization first (HashMap). If memory is concern, convert to bottom-up DP table.

star.s: Interview: coin change problem — minimum coins to make amount.
star.a: dp[amount] = min coins. For each coin, dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i-coin]+1). O(amount × coins) time.

terms.what: Sliding window maintains a window of elements and slides it across the array. Expand right to grow, shrink left to reduce. For subarray/substring problems.
terms.why: Brute force for 'max subarray of size K' is O(n×k). Sliding window reuses previous work — O(n). Key technique for string/subarray problems.
terms.how: int left=0, sum=0, max=0; for(int right=0; right<arr.length; right++) { sum+=arr[right]; while(sum>k) sum-=arr[left++]; max=Math.max(max, right-left+1); }
terms.where: Maximum subarray sum of size K, longest substring without repeating characters, minimum window substring.
terms.when: Use sliding window for contiguous subarray/substring problems. Variable window when size can change, fixed window for fixed size K.
terms.issue: Off-by-one in window size calculation — common in fixed window. Forgetting to shrink left pointer.
terms.fix: Trace through small example manually. Track [left, right] inclusive bounds.

star.s: Interview: longest substring without repeating characters.
star.a: Sliding window with HashSet. right expands, left shrinks when duplicate found. O(n).

terms.what: Precompute prefix sums: preSum[i] = preSum[i-1] + arr[i]. Then range sum arr[l..r] = preSum[r] - preSum[l-1] in O(1). After O(n) precomputation.
terms.why: Range sum queries: brute O(n) per query. Prefix sum: O(1) per query after O(n) build. Enables subarray sum problems.
terms.how: int[] pre = new int[n+1]; for(int i=1; i<=n; i++) pre[i] = pre[i-1] + arr[i-1]; int rangeSum = pre[r+1] - pre[l]; // sum from l to r inclusive.
terms.where: Range sum queries, subarray sum equals K, counting subarrays with sum divisible by K.
terms.when: Use prefix sum when multiple range sum queries are needed on same array. Also with HashMap for subarray sum problems.
terms.issue: 0-indexing vs 1-indexing confusion for prefix array. Off-by-one in range calculation.
terms.fix: Use 1-indexed prefix array (size n+1) for cleaner boundary handling.

star.a: Prefix sum + HashMap. For each prefix sum, check if (currentSum - K) seen before. O(n).

terms.what: Maximum subarray sum in O(n). Track maxEndingHere (max sum ending at current index) and maxSoFar (global max). maxEndingHere = max(arr[i], maxEndingHere + arr[i]).
terms.why: Brute force O(n²). Kadane's O(n). Classic problem — appears in many variations.
terms.how: int maxSoFar=arr[0], maxEndingHere=arr[0]; for(int i=1; i<n; i++) { maxEndingHere = Math.max(arr[i], maxEndingHere+arr[i]); maxSoFar = Math.max(maxSoFar, maxEndingHere); }
terms.where: Maximum subarray, buy/sell stock, maximum product subarray (variant).
terms.when: Whenever you need maximum contiguous subarray sum.
terms.issue: All negative array: correct answer is the least negative number, not 0. Initialize with arr[0], not 0.
terms.fix: Initialize maxSoFar = maxEndingHere = arr[0], loop from index 1.

terms.what: Arrays.sort(arr): sorts array in-place. For objects: TimSort O(n log n). For primitives: dual-pivot Quicksort. Arrays.binarySearch(arr, key): binary search on SORTED array. Returns index or -(insertion point)-1.
Fast pointer moves 2 steps, slow pointer moves 1 step. If there's a cycle, they meet. To find cycle start: reset slow to head, both advance 1 step — they meet at cycle start.
terms.why: O(n) space-free cycle detection. Also finds the middle of a linked list: when fast reaches end, slow is at middle.
terms.how: Cycle detect: slow=head, fast=head; while(fast!=null && fast.next!=null) { slow=slow.next; fast=fast.next.next; if(slow==fast) cycleDetected; } Middle: when fast==null, slow=middle.
terms.where: Linked list cycle detection, finding middle node, Leetcode: Linked List Cycle (141, 142), Middle of Linked List (876).
terms.when: Use two pointers whenever cycle detection or middle-finding on linked list is needed.
terms.issue: Null pointer access when checking fast.next.next without null guard.
terms.fix: Always check: while(fast != null && fast.next != null) before moving.

star.s: Interview: detect cycle in linked list.
star.a: Floyd's cycle detection. Fast+slow pointers meet if cycle exists.

terms.what: Reverse a singly linked list iteratively using three pointers: prev, curr, next. Each step: save next, point curr to prev, advance both.
terms.why: Classic interview question. Basis for many linked list problems: palindrome check, reverse in groups, etc.
terms.how: Node prev=null, curr=head, next=null; while(curr!=null) { next=curr.next; curr.next=prev; prev=curr; curr=next; } return prev; // new head.
terms.where: Leetcode #206. Foundation for: reverse in k-groups, check palindrome, rotate list.
terms.when: Whenever reversing a linked list or part of it. O(n) time, O(1) space.
terms.issue: Losing the rest of the list by not saving next before reassigning curr.next.
terms.fix: Always: next = curr.next BEFORE curr.next = prev.

star.a: Three pointer iterative. prev/curr/next. While loop. Return prev.

terms.what: Merge two sorted lists: compare heads, take smaller, advance that pointer. Dummy node: create ListNode dummy=new ListNode(0); build result list with dummy as anchor.
terms.why: Dummy node simplifies edge cases — no special handling for empty list or first node. Common technique for list construction.
terms.how: ListNode dummy=new ListNode(0), curr=dummy; while(l1!=null && l2!=null) { if(l1.val<=l2.val) { curr.next=l1; l1=l1.next; } else { curr.next=l2; l2=l2.next; } curr=curr.next; } curr.next = l1!=null?l1:l2; return dummy.next;
terms.where: Leetcode #21 (Merge Two Sorted Lists), merge sort, merge K sorted lists.
terms.when: Use dummy node whenever building a result linked list from scratch.

terms.what: Preorder: root→left→right (copy, serialize). Inorder: left→root→right (BST sorted output). Postorder: left→right→root (delete, calculate size from bottom).
terms.why: Each traversal order serves different purposes. Inorder on BST = sorted sequence — unique property.
terms.how: Preorder: visit(node); dfs(node.left); dfs(node.right). Inorder: dfs(node.left); visit(node); dfs(node.right). Postorder: dfs(node.left); dfs(node.right); visit(node).
terms.where: Tree serialization/deserialization, expression trees, file system traversal, BST operations.
terms.when: Inorder for BST validation and sorted output. Preorder for tree cloning. Postorder for tree deletion and bottom-up calculations.
terms.issue: Recursion for large trees = StackOverflow. Iterative version needed for deep trees.
terms.fix: Use explicit Stack for iterative DFS. Or Java 21 virtual threads for very deep recursion.

star.s: Interview: given BST, verify it's valid.
star.a: Inorder traversal. BST inorder should be strictly increasing. Compare each node with previous in traversal.

terms.what: BFS uses a Queue. Process level by level. Queue contains all nodes of current level. For each node, add its children to queue.
terms.why: Shortest path in unweighted trees, level-by-level processing, finding minimum depth, printing tree level by level.
terms.how: Queue<TreeNode> q = new ArrayDeque<>(); q.offer(root); while(!q.isEmpty()) { int levelSize=q.size(); for(int i=0; i<levelSize; i++) { TreeNode n=q.poll(); process(n); if(n.left!=null) q.offer(n.left); if(n.right!=null) q.offer(n.right); } }
terms.where: Leetcode #102 (Level Order), min depth, right side view, zigzag traversal, graph BFS.
terms.when: Level order for level-by-level processing. BFS for shortest path in unweighted graph.
terms.issue: Not tracking level boundaries — cannot separate levels.
terms.fix: Use levelSize = queue.size() at start of each level to know how many nodes are in current level.

star.s: Interview: right side view of binary tree.
star.a: BFS level order. For each level, take last node. O(n).

terms.what: LCA of two nodes p and q: the deepest node that is an ancestor of both. For BST: if p and q both smaller than root, go left; both larger, go right; else root is LCA.
terms.why: Common tree problem. Used in path distance calculation, tree distance queries.
terms.how: General tree: if(root==null||root==p||root==q) return root; Node left=lca(root.left,p,q); Node right=lca(root.right,p,q); return left!=null&&right!=null?root:left!=null?left:right;
terms.where: Leetcode #236 (LCA of Binary Tree), #235 (LCA of BST), tree path queries.
terms.when: Return the node when found. Combine results from left and right recursion.
terms.issue: BST LCA algorithm doesn't work for general trees — must use general approach.
terms.fix: General tree LCA: recurse both sides, return root if both found in different subtrees.

star.a: Recurse. If current == p or q, return it. Combine left and right results — if both non-null, current is LCA.

terms.what: Trie is a tree for string operations. Each node represents one character. Root is empty. Words share prefixes. Insert/Search/StartsWith all O(L) where L = word length.
terms.why: Prefix matching (autocomplete, dictionary), word search, IP routing tables. Much faster than HashMap for prefix queries.
terms.how: class TrieNode { TrieNode[] children = new TrieNode[26]; boolean isEnd; } insert: for each char, children[c-'a'] = new node if null. search: walk nodes, return isEnd.
terms.where: Autocomplete, spell check, word search in grid, prefix matching, longest common prefix.
terms.when: Use Trie for prefix-based queries. HashSet is faster for exact lookups — Trie shines for prefix operations.
terms.issue: 26-child array wastes memory for sparse alphabets. HashMap<Character, TrieNode> children saves memory but slower.
terms.fix: Use HashMap children for non-ASCII or variable-size alphabets. Array for ASCII- only.

star.a: Trie. Insert all words. startsWith(prefix): walk prefix path, collect all words below that node with DFS.

terms.what: DFS: explore deeply before backtracking — uses Stack/recursion. BFS: explore level by level — uses Queue. Topological sort: ordering of DAG nodes where all edges point forward — BFS (Kahn's) or DFS postorder.
terms.why: DFS for cycle detection, path finding, topological sort. BFS for shortest path in unweighted graph, connected components.
terms.how: Kahn's topological sort: compute in-degree for all nodes. Add all 0-in-degree nodes to queue. BFS: process, reduce neighbor in-degrees, add newly 0-in-degree to queue.
terms.where: Course prerequisites, build dependency order, task scheduling, detect cycles in directed graph.
terms.when: Topological sort: schedule tasks with dependencies. If result has fewer nodes than graph = cycle exists.
terms.issue: Topological sort on cyclic graph — Kahn's algorithm detects it: result.size() < numNodes means cycle exists.
terms.fix: After Kahn's algorithm: check if result.size() == numNodes. If not: cycle exists.

star.s: Interview: course schedule — can all courses be finished?
star.a: Topological sort (Kahn's). If result size < numCourses, cycle exists, return false.

terms.what: Shortest path in weighted graph with non-negative edges. PriorityQueue (min-heap) + relaxation. O((V+E) log V) with binary heap.
terms.why: Shortest path with weights. BFS only works for unweighted graphs.
terms.how: PriorityQueue<int[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>(Comparator.comparingInt(a->a[1])); // [node, dist]. Visited set. For each node, update distances to neighbors if shorter path found.
terms.where: Navigation (Google Maps), network routing, game pathfinding.
terms.when: Use Dijkstra for non-negative weighted shortest path. Bellman-Ford if negative weights exist.
terms.issue: Dijkstra doesn't work with negative edge weights — Bellman-Ford required.
terms.fix: Check for negative edges. Use Bellman-Ford if present.

star.s: Interview: cheapest flight path with weight.

terms.what: Union-Find tracks connected components. find(x): root of x's component. union(x,y): merge components. With path compression + union by rank: near O(1) amortized.
